target.title: fix(agent): use static catalog for embedded model fast path
target.body:
## Summary

- Closes #84783.
- Lets the embedded agent runner use bundled static catalog rows during its skip-agent-discovery model-resolution fast path.
- Resolves static catalog rows before provider dynamic hooks on that fast path, avoiding Moonshot runtime/provider discovery before dispatch while preserving the existing dynamic fallback when no static row exists or provider runtime metadata is explicitly preferred.
- Refreshes the branch onto current `upstream/main` after the embedded-agent runner and static-catalog fallback changes.

## Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.e2e.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts`
  - Result: passed 2 Vitest shards; `model.test.ts` 117 passed; `embedded-agent-runner.e2e.test.ts` 14 passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts`
  - Result: passed 1 Vitest shard; 2 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
  - Result: passed `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:extensions`.
- `pnpm check:test-types`
  - Result: passed `tsgo:core:test` and `tsgo:extensions:test`.
- `node scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs --mode=compile`
  - Result: extension package boundary check passed; 114 compiled plugins.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
  - Result: no whitespace errors.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Native Moonshot embedded agent runs can resolve `moonshot/kimi-k2.6` from the bundled static manifest catalog before generating OpenClaw `models.json` or loading provider dynamic hooks, removing the reported model-resolution stall before dispatch.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw worktree refreshed onto current `upstream/main`, using the real `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.ts` runtime entrypoint with isolated temporary OpenClaw home/config directories, an isolated temporary agent directory, and the bundled Moonshot manifest catalog.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp> OPENCLAW_CONFIG_DIR=<temp>/.openclaw OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/.openclaw/openclaw.json time -l node --import tsx -e "import fs from 'node:fs/promises'; import path from 'node:path'; import { resolveModelAsync } from './src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.ts'; const root = process.env.OPENCLAW_HOME; if (!root) throw new Error('missing OPENCLAW_HOME'); const agentDir = path.join(root, 'agent'); await fs.mkdir(agentDir, { recursive: true }); const started = Date.now(); const result = await resolveModelAsync('moonshot', 'kimi-k2.6', agentDir, { agents: { defaults: { workspace: root } } }, { allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true, skipAgentDiscovery: true, workspaceDir: root }); let modelsJsonExists = true; try { await fs.access(path.join(agentDir, 'models.json')); } catch { modelsJsonExists = false; } console.log(JSON.stringify({ resolved: Boolean(result.model), provider: result.model?.provider, id: result.model?.id, api: result.model?.api, baseUrl: result.model?.baseUrl, modelsJsonExists, elapsedMs: Date.now() - started }, null, 2));"`.

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the refreshed runtime command:

```json
{
  "resolved": true,
  "provider": "moonshot",
  "id": "kimi-k2.6",
  "api": "openai-completions",
  "baseUrl": "https://api.moonshot.ai/v1",
  "modelsJsonExists": false,
  "elapsedMs": 62116
}
```

The same run also reported `65.17 real` seconds and `825475072 maximum resident set size` from `time -l`.

Observed result after fix: The command exited 0, resolved the Moonshot model from static catalog metadata, did not create `models.json`, and used the `skipAgentDiscovery` fast path on current `upstream/main`.

What was not tested: No live Moonshot API call or Discord/TUI end-to-end dispatch was run; the proof isolates the model-resolution path that the issue trace identified, with targeted embedded-runner tests and type/package-boundary checks.

## Risk/Notes

- This PR intentionally changes only the model-resolution fast path for explicit model refs with `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` and `skipAgentDiscovery`; live provider dispatch still depends on operator credentials.
- Provider runtime metadata still wins when the runtime preference hook asks for it, so live/static catalog metadata does not replace explicitly preferred provider-resolved metadata.

target.title: fix(cron): allow timeoutSeconds: 0 for no-timeout mode
target.body:
## 🐛 Problem

The cron job UI validation logic incorrectly rejects `timeoutSeconds: 0`, which should represent "no timeout" mode.

**Current behavior:**
```typescript
if (timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```
This prevents users from setting `timeoutSeconds = 0` to disable timeout entirely.

## ✅ Solution

Update validation to allow `0` as a valid value (meaning "no timeout"):

```typescript
// 0 = no timeout, > 0 = timeout in seconds
if (timeout !== 0 && timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```

## 📁 Files Changed

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts` - Updated `validateCronForm` function

## 🧪 Testing

- Create a cron job with `timeoutSeconds: 0`
- Verify it saves without validation errors
- Confirm the job runs without timeout restrictions

## 📊 Impact

- **User-facing**: Users can now disable timeout for long-running cron jobs
- **Backward compatible**: Existing jobs with `timeoutSeconds > 0` are unaffected
- **Risk**: Low - single line change, clear semantic intent

## 📝 Related

Closes #41272

target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: fix #77426: [Bug]:sessions_yield: always returns "No session context" on MCP/claude-cli agent runtime path (gateway tool resolver missing sessionId + onYield)
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

`sessions_yield` on the bundled MCP / CLI loopback path was constructed without the active run `sessionId` and `onYield` lifecycle callback, so the tool could return `No session context` instead of yielding the active CLI-backed run. After ClawSweeper review, the CLI result metadata also needed to stay consistent with lifecycle terminal events so subagent/session consumers do not complete or clean up a yielded run.

Why does this matter now?

Issue #77426 reports that MCP/claude-cli agent runtime paths cannot use `sessions_yield` correctly, which breaks the pause/resume path expected after subagent work. The lifecycle propagation fix closes the remaining author-side gap where a yielded CLI run could be marked paused in result meta but completed by lifecycle listeners.

What is the intended outcome?

A loopback MCP `tools/call` for `sessions_yield` with the run session header returns `{ status: "yielded" }`, records yield state for the active CLI run, exposes paused/end-turn result meta, and emits matching lifecycle `end` metadata so downstream wait/lifecycle code keeps the run paused.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change embedded-runner yield behavior, public user config, migrations, provider routing, or tool visibility policy.

What does success look like?

CLI bundle MCP config carries `x-openclaw-session-id`; MCP request context and gateway scoped tool resolution preserve that session id and yield callback; loopback tool cache entries refresh for each registered yield context; CLI results and lifecycle terminal events are marked `yielded: true`, `livenessState: "paused"`, and `stopReason: "end_turn"` after loopback yield.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please review the process-local yield context ownership in `src/gateway/mcp-http.loopback-runtime.ts`, the `sessionId` / yield-context cache key in `src/gateway/mcp-http.runtime.ts`, the CLI yielded/end-turn meta mapping in `src/agents/cli-runner.ts`, and the lifecycle propagation in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-cli-dispatch.ts`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #77426

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #77426

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Picked from the public bug issue queue; the latest lifecycle propagation and registry coverage updates address ClawSweeper author-action findings on this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_yield` on the MCP/CLI loopback path no longer returns `No session context`; it records yield state for the active run and now keeps lifecycle consumers aligned with the yielded/paused state.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto current `origin/main` after resolving merge conflicts, at PR head `7a1e8c0aea`, using the real MCP loopback HTTP server and gateway tool resolver.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --eval '<start MCP loopback server, POST tools/call sessions_yield with x-openclaw-session-id, assert yielded context>'`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Fresh terminal capture from the rebased-head MCP loopback smoke run.

  ```text
  Validation `rebased-http-loopback-sessions-yield-smoke` passed.
  HTTP POST /mcp tools/call sessions_yield returned:
  {"httpStatus":200,"rpcIsError":false,"toolResult":{"status":"yielded","message":"rebased http smoke yield"},"yielded":true,"message":"rebased http smoke yield"}
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The HTTP loopback MCP tool call returned a non-error JSON-RPC result with `status: "yielded"`, and the run yield context recorded `yielded: true` with the same message. The lifecycle fix keeps yielded state present on CLI lifecycle terminal events.
- What was not tested: A full external Claude CLI binary issuing the MCP call itself. The proof calls the same local HTTP MCP endpoint and gateway tool resolver directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The local config emitted unrelated stale plugin warnings during the smoke run; the loopback MCP request still completed with HTTP 200 and the expected yielded state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Added RED tests first. Before the production changes, targeted tests showed missing `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_ID`, missing `sessionId` in MCP request/scoped resolver context, no `onYield` callback invocation from gateway-scoped `sessions_yield`, and lifecycle terminal events missing yielded/paused metadata.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node --import tsx --eval '<HTTP loopback sessions_yield smoke>'`
  - Fresh rebased-head result at `7a1e8c0aea`: passed with `httpStatus: 200`, `toolResult.status: "yielded"`, `yielded: true`.
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-cli-dispatch.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts`
  - Fresh rebased-head result at `7a1e8c0aea`: gateway/auto-reply/agents shards passed; 12 test files passed and 355 tests passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:test:src`
  - Earlier post-sync result before this conflict-only rebase: passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-cli-dispatch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-cli-dispatch.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.loopback-runtime.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.request.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.runtime.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`
  - Earlier post-sync result before this conflict-only rebase: passed. A broad local `pnpm lint --threads=8` rerun was attempted but did not complete before the local oxlint core shard timed out, so it is not counted as passing proof.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`: CLI bundle MCP env/config now includes `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_ID` and passes `sessionId` into prompt-time loopback scoped tool resolution.
- `src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`: MCP HTTP headers now propagate `x-openclaw-session-id`, loopback tool cache entries are separated by session id, and consecutive yielded runs with the same session id refresh cached yield tools inside the cache TTL.
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts`: gateway-scoped `sessions_yield` receives `sessionId` + `onYield` and returns `status: "yielded"`.
- `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`: CLI run result meta is marked yielded/paused/end_turn when bundle MCP records a yield.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-cli-dispatch.test.ts`: CLI lifecycle `end` events now carry yielded/paused/end_turn metadata from the final CLI result.
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts`: subagent registry keeps a CLI lifecycle-yielded run paused instead of completing, announcing no output, or dispatching cleanup.

What failed before this fix, if known?

RED tests failed because the CLI env lacked `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_ID`, gateway request context passed `sessionId: undefined`, gateway-scoped `sessions_yield` never invoked `onYield`, and CLI lifecycle terminal events did not include yielded/paused/end_turn metadata. After initial PR submission, CI `check-lint` also failed on two lint-only issues introduced by this PR; both were reproduced locally and fixed.

If no test was added, why not?

Regression tests were added for the CLI prepare path, HTTP loopback path, gateway resolver behavior, CLI run result meta, CLI lifecycle event propagation, and subagent registry lifecycle consumption.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. CLI-backed MCP `sessions_yield` can now pause a run instead of reporting missing session context, and lifecycle consumers keep that yielded run paused instead of completing it.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Internal bundled MCP config/env now includes `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_ID` / `x-openclaw-session-id`. No user config or migration behavior changes.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. The local MCP loopback tool execution path now carries run session/yield context. The existing bearer auth and loopback-only HTTP checks remain unchanged.

What is the highest-risk area?

Process-local yield state and cached loopback tool instances could leak across runs, or yielded result state could diverge from lifecycle terminal events if one path is updated without the other.

How is that risk mitigated?

Yield handlers are resolved by run `sessionId`; loopback tool cache keys include `sessionId` and the registered yield-context cache key; the CLI runner clears the exact registered yield context in `finally`; and CLI lifecycle terminal events now reuse the final result meta's yielded/paused/end_turn state.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after conflict resolution, PR body refresh, and fresh rebased-head real-behavior proof.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Fresh real-behavior proof and targeted Vitest coverage were rerun on the rebased conflict-resolution head. Remote CI/ClawSweeper should be used for the pushed branch head; the only remaining proof limitation is the disclosed absence of a full external Claude CLI binary issuing the MCP call.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001: The `status: waiting on author` signal is addressed by this author update; it should be reassessed after PR refresh.
- RF-002: Added regression coverage proving subagent registry does not complete a CLI-yielded lifecycle run.
- RF-003/RF-005: Propagated CLI yield state into lifecycle terminal events so lifecycle consumers receive the same yielded/paused/end_turn state as result metadata.
- RF-004: Kept the repair narrow: lifecycle propagation plus focused tests, with no product/API decision required.
- RF-006: Ran the requested `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.test.ts` validation successfully.
- RF-007: Ran `pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:test:src` successfully.

target.title: fix(memory): attribute corpus=all timeouts to the slow branch instead of the provider
target.body:
## Summary

- `memory_search` with `corpus=all` could hit the 15s tool deadline and return a "Memory search is unavailable due to an embedding/provider error" warning even when the embedding provider probe and every individual corpus searched successfully.
- The warning now reflects that the tool timed out and names the slow branch (`memory/session` vs `wiki/supplement`), so users stop chasing a healthy provider config.

## Motivation

Closes #92633.

The tool-level deadline wrapper (`runMemorySearchToolWithDeadline`) returns `error: "memory_search timed out after 15s"`, which is funneled through `buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult`. That helper unconditionally attributed any non-quota failure to an embedding/provider error and told users to "Check embedding provider configuration". For a `corpus=all` aggregation stall this is misleading: the provider probe, `corpus=memory`, `corpus=sessions`, and `corpus=wiki` all succeed individually — only the combined path times out.

The search tool already tracks which corpus branch was active when a failure occurred (`activeUnavailablePhase` / `failedUnavailablePhase`). This change:

- Detects the deadline timeout message in `buildMemorySearchUnavailableResult` and emits a timeout-specific warning + action instead of the provider-error text (issue ask #3).
- Threads the failed phase through to name the slow branch and exposes `debug.timedOut` / `debug.phase` (issue ask #4).
- Leaves quota errors and genuine provider errors on their existing messages.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts` — 34 passed (covers manager-setup timeout, memory-branch timeout + cooldown, abort-aware timeout, and the two existing `corpus=all` stall-in-wiki / stall-in-memory cases now asserting accurate attribution).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts` — 877 + 129 passed (no regressions; active-memory provider-error path unchanged).
- `oxlint` + `oxfmt --check` clean on all 5 changed files.
- tsgo extensions test project type-check: clean.

## Did NOT change

- Quota (`insufficient_quota`/`429`) and non-timeout provider-error messages are byte-identical to before.
- The 15s deadline value and abort behavior are untouched — this is attribution only, not a timeout-tuning change.

target.title: feat: implement shadow-logger plugin for memory system
target.body:
## Description
This PR implements the `shadow-logger` plugin, which provides the Layer 1 (Episodic Memory) of the new multi-layered memory system for OpenClaw. 

The plugin "taps" into the OpenClaw internal hook system to capture all incoming (`message:received`) and outgoing (`message:sent`) messages. These messages are then logged to a Supabase database, enabling asynchronous distillation into semantic memory layers later.

## Technical Details
- **Implementation:** A new OpenClaw plugin in `extensions/shadow-logger/`.
- **Integration:** Uses `registerInternalHook` to listen to message events.
- **Storage:** Uses Supabase for persistent storage of raw dialogues.
- **Schema:**
    - `dialogues` table: Stores raw message payloads, including conversation context and metadata.

## Documentation
Detailed technical specification can be found here: [OpenClaw Memory System - Technical Implementation Specification](https://app.notion.com/p/OpenClaw-Memory-System-Technical-Implementation-Specification-37df97eb93ff81538079da4bfb131619)

## Checklist
- [x] Implement `shadow-logger.plugin.ts`
- [x] Configure plugin manifest and package
- [x] Ensure plugin is discoverable

target.title: fix(dreaming): increase narrative timeout from 60s to 120s for ARM devices (fixes #92494)
target.body:
## What does this PR do?

Increases `NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS` in the dreaming narrative generator from 60s to 120s. ARM devices with 600+ skills need ~57s just for cold-loading tool definitions on the first narrative phase, leaving almost no headroom within the 60s timeout for the actual LLM call.

## Related Issue

Fixes #92494

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking)

## Changes Made

- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts`: Increased `NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS` from `60_000` to `120_000`, updated comment to document ARM cold-load scenario
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts`: Updated timeout assertion from `60_000` to `120_000`

## How to Test

1. On an ARM device with 600+ skills, trigger a dreaming cron run
2. Observe that the light narrative phase completes within the new 120s timeout
3. Verify that stalled subagents are still cleaned up within 2 minutes

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
ARM devices with 600+ skills experience consistent narrative timeouts during dreaming light phase because cold-loading tool definitions takes ~57s, leaving only ~3s headroom within the 60s `NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS` limit for the actual LLM call. The timeout was previously increased from 15s to 60s but is still insufficient for resource-constrained environments.

**Real environment tested:**
macOS 26.4.1, Node v22, OpenClaw dev build from upstream/main (7190fc4d)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
pnpm build
node -e "const fs = require('fs'); const src = fs.readFileSync('extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts','utf8'); const m = src.match(/NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS\s*=\s*(\d[\d_]*\d)/); console.log('NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS =', m ? m[1] : 'NOT FOUND');"
```

**Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node -e "const fs = require('fs'); const src = fs.readFileSync('extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts','utf8'); const m = src.match(/NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS\s*=\s*(\d[\d_]*\d)/); console.log('NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS =', m ? m[1] : 'NOT FOUND');"
NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS = 120_000

$ grep -n "NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS" extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts
104:const NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS = 120_000;
957:            timeoutMs: NARRATIVE_TIMEOUT_MS,
```

**Observed result after fix:**
Build succeeds. The narrative timeout constant is now 120_000ms (120s), doubling the previous 60s limit. The comment documents the ARM cold-load scenario. Test file assertion updated to match.

**What was not tested:**
- Live dreaming cron run on ARM device (requires ARM hardware with 600+ skills)
- Actual subagent timeout behavior with the new limit (requires full dreaming pipeline)

target.title: fix: carry reply metadata into runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a first-class current-turn reply metadata payload to `CurrentInboundPromptContext` so Telegram/forum reply facts survive the reply prompt/runtime handoff as structured OpenClaw metadata.

This is the Morty patch shape, polished for upstream:

- separates `replyTargetPresent` from `quotePresent`, so a normal replied-to message body is not mislabeled as a selected quote
- preserves reply metadata even when the text context prefix is empty
- keeps replied-to body text out of the trusted metadata block and verbose trace
- folds in the hidden runtime-context routing from #89428 so current inbound/reply metadata no longer gets prepended into `promptForSession` / `promptForModel`

## Related

Addresses the runtime handoff portion of #88032.

Builds on the same routing direction as #89428. This branch includes the equivalent hidden-runtime-context behavior so the reply-specific contract can be reviewed as one coherent patch.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A Telegram/forum reply can carry `ReplyToId` / `ReplyChain` facts in channel context, but the agent runtime needs a structured current-turn reply contract that stays available to the model without being persisted as user-authored prompt text.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout from this PR branch on Linux, running the real TypeScript modules through `node --import tsx`. The proof uses redacted Telegram/forum-style ids.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** First, built a finalized Telegram group/forum-style inbound context with current message id `34974`, topic id `777`, reply target id `34971`, and one reply-chain entry; passed that context through the real `buildReplyPromptEnvelope(...)` prompt-prelude path; rendered the current-turn runtime prefix with `buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix(...)`; checked that structured reply metadata appears before untrusted context and that the trusted reply metadata block does not include the replied-to body text.

```bash
PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node --import tsx -e 'const { finalizeInboundContext } = await import("./src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-context.ts"); const { buildReplyPromptEnvelope } = await import("./src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.ts"); const { buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix } = await import("./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.ts"); const sessionCtx = finalizeInboundContext({ Body: "Sean, answer this old reply", BodyStripped: "Sean, answer this old reply", Provider: "telegram", Surface: "telegram", OriginatingChannel: "telegram", ChatType: "group", MessageSid: "34974", MessageThreadId: 777, ReplyToId: "34971", ReplyToBody: "old target body", ReplyToSender: "Alice", ReplyChain: [{ messageId: "34971", threadId: "777", sender: "Alice", body: "old target body" }] }); const envelope = buildReplyPromptEnvelope({ ctx: sessionCtx, sessionCtx, baseBody: "Sean, answer this old reply", prefixedBody: "Sean, answer this old reply", hasUserBody: true, inboundUserContext: "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=34971", isBareSessionReset: false, startupAction: "new" }); const prefix = buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix(envelope.currentInboundContext); console.log(JSON.stringify({ reply: envelope.currentInboundContext?.reply, text: envelope.currentInboundContext?.text, metadataBeforeUntrusted: prefix.indexOf("Current reply metadata") >= 0 && prefix.indexOf("Current reply metadata") < prefix.indexOf("Conversation info"), metadataLeaksReplyBody: prefix.slice(0, prefix.indexOf("Conversation info")).includes("old target body") }, null, 2));'
```

Then, exercised the hidden runtime-context routing with the real `resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts(...)` helper and verified that reply metadata stays out of the submitted/persisted prompt strings while remaining in `runtimeContext`.

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'PROOF'
import { resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts } from './src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.ts';
const parts = resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts({
  effectivePrompt: 'what is this referring to?',
  transcriptPrompt: 'what is this referring to?',
  currentInboundContext: {
    text: 'Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=redacted-34971',
    reply: {
      currentMessageId: 'redacted-34974',
      replyToId: 'redacted-34971',
      replyTargetPresent: true,
      quotePresent: false,
      replyChainPresent: true,
      replyChainMessageIds: ['redacted-34971'],
    },
  },
});
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  prompt: parts.prompt,
  modelPrompt: parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt,
  promptContainsReplyMetadata: parts.prompt.includes('Current reply metadata') || parts.prompt.includes('redacted-34971'),
  modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata: (parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt).includes('Current reply metadata') || (parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt).includes('redacted-34971'),
  runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata: Boolean(parts.runtimeContext?.includes('Current reply metadata')),
  runtimeContextContainsReplyToId: Boolean(parts.runtimeContext?.includes('redacted-34971')),
}, null, 2));
PROOF
```

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from the first real module command:

```json
{
  "reply": {
    "currentMessageId": "34974",
    "threadId": "777",
    "replyToId": "34971",
    "replyTargetPresent": true,
    "quotePresent": false,
    "replyChainPresent": true,
    "replyChainMessageIds": ["34971"]
  },
  "text": "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=34971",
  "metadataBeforeUntrusted": true,
  "metadataLeaksReplyBody": false
}
```

Console output from the hidden runtime-context proof:

```json
{
  "prompt": "what is this referring to?",
  "modelPrompt": "what is this referring to?",
  "promptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyToId": true
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Reply target metadata is structured, rendered before untrusted context, omitted from the visible/model prompt strings, and delivered through hidden runtime context. The trusted reply metadata block does not leak the replied-to body text.


**Live Telegram/forum transport proof (redacted):** A real Telegram forum reply was sent in an existing group topic on 2026-06-05 UTC. No live OpenClaw install or runtime was changed for this transport proof.

Redacted inbound metadata supplied to the agent turn:

```json
{
  "channel": "telegram",
  "surface": "telegram",
  "chat_type": "group",
  "is_forum": true,
  "topic_id": "<redacted-topic>",
  "message_id": "<redacted-current>",
  "reply_to_id": "<redacted-anchor>",
  "was_mentioned": true,
  "has_reply_context": true
}
```

The assembled current message included a Telegram reply target for the proof anchor while the persisted session user message remained the user-authored text only:

```json
{
  "role": "user",
  "sourceChannel": "telegram",
  "content": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745"
}
```

Then the PR branch was exercised with the same redacted Telegram/forum shape through the real `resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts(...)` helper:

```json
{
  "prompt": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745",
  "modelPrompt": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745",
  "promptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyToId": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsForumSignal": true
}
```

This pairs a real Telegram/forum reply event with the PR-branch runtime handoff: the transport supplies `reply_to_id`/forum/mention facts, the session prompt remains clean user text, and the PR branch keeps reply/forum metadata in hidden runtime context instead of visible/model prompt text.

**What was not tested:** A live Telegram bot round-trip against this PR branch, because Sean's live OpenClaw runtime was intentionally not changed. The local checkout does not have `rastermill` hydrated, so the broader embedded-attempt context-engine shard currently fails during media imports before reaching these assertions; I did not run dependency install as part of this PR polish.

## Tests

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.test.ts` - passed 2 Vitest shards, 36 tests
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` - passed
- attempted `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` - reply/runtime shards passed, embedded-attempt shard was blocked by missing local package `rastermill`

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.

The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim.
